1.synthesis: Paul says it is superfluous to write about the ministry to the saints — he knows the Corinthians' readiness, and has boasted of it to the Macedonians: 'Achaia has been ready since last year' (v.2). But he has sent the brothers ahead, so that the collection would already be prepared when he arrives with Macedonians in tow — otherwise his boasting would prove empty and the Corinthians would be shamed (vv.3–4). He therefore urges the brothers to go in advance and arrange the promised gift, so that it would be ready as a willing gift and not as an exaction (v.5).
1.historical_context: Chrysostom observes a characteristic Pauline rhetorical move: 'Though he had said so much about it, he says here, it is superfluous for me to write to you. And his wisdom is shown not only in this, that though he had said so much about it, he saith, it is superfluous, but in that he yet again speaketh of it.' The feigned redundancy is itself a form of persuasion — by saying it is needless to urge them, Paul implies confidence in their character while still urging them. The boast to the Macedonians that Achaia was ready served the additional purpose of having motivated the Macedonians' own extraordinary giving (8:1–5) — the Corinthian example had already done real work in the broader apostolic community. Paul's anxiety that they not be 'shamed' at his arrival reflects the honor-shame dynamics of the ancient Mediterranean world.
1.literary_structure: This brief section functions as the bridge between the theological argument of chapter 8 and the motivational argument of vv.6–15. It provides the practical and social logic for the advance sending of Titus: not compulsion but preparation. The key distinction in v.5 — 'a willing gift (eulogia, blessing) and not an exaction (pleonexia, greed)' — sets up the theology of cheerful giving in v.7. The gift must have the quality of a eulogia (literally a speaking-well, a blessing) — something given with glad intention — rather than the character of extraction.
1.theological_application: The distinction between 'blessing' and 'exaction' in v.5 is theologically precise. A true gift is a blessing — it flows from the abundance of the giver's heart and benefits the receiver. Coerced giving, by contrast, has the character of pleonexia (greed/exaction) even when the extracted money goes to worthy causes, because the relationship between giver and recipient has been corrupted by compulsion. This distinction shapes the entire framework of Christian generosity: the Spirit produces genuine gifts; external pressure produces something else. Matthew Henry observes that Paul's advance sending of the brothers 'shows that he used proper means to have things well managed and conducted in an orderly manner' — wise administration serves genuine generosity rather than undermining it.
1.christology: The eulogia that the collection should be is itself a participation in the pattern of God's own speaking-well: the blessing that God spoke over Abraham (Genesis 12:2–3), which has come to the nations in Christ (Galatians 3:14). The collection from the Gentiles for the Jewish poor in Jerusalem is a concrete enactment of that blessing flowing from Gentile to Jew through the gospel — a reversal of the direction of blessing from Romans 15:27, where Paul notes that if the Gentiles have shared in spiritual blessings from the Jewish people, they ought to be of service in material things. The collection is thus not merely charitable but eschatological: it embodies the unity of Jew and Gentile in the one body of Christ.

6.synthesis: Paul lays out the economy of giving: 'Whoever sows sparingly will also reap sparingly, and whoever sows bountifully will also reap bountifully' (v.6). Each one must give as he has decided in his heart — not reluctantly or under compulsion, for God loves a cheerful giver (v.7). God is able to make all grace abound toward them, so that having all sufficiency in all things they may abound in every good work (v.8). The psalm quotation — 'He has distributed freely, he has given to the poor; his righteousness endures forever' (v.9) — is applied to the generous giver. God who supplies seed to the sower and bread for food will supply and multiply the seed and increase the harvest of righteousness (v.10). The enriched givers produce thanksgiving to God through the collection, which overflows in many thanksgivings (vv.11–12), and ultimately leads the recipients to glorify God (vv.13–14). The section — and the whole of the collection argument — closes with a doxology: 'Thanks be to God for his inexpressible gift!' (v.15).
6.historical_context: The sowing-and-reaping metaphor was widely used in both Jewish wisdom literature and Greco-Roman moral philosophy to describe the returns of generosity. Matthew Henry notes that Paul 'would have the Corinthians give bountifully, and this was intimated in that the apostle expected a liberal contribution.' But Paul transforms the agricultural analogy: it is not merely a moral principle about natural returns but a theological claim about divine multiplication. The citation of Psalm 112:9 in v.9 — 'his righteousness endures forever' — applies to the generous person the language normally used of God's own character, suggesting that human generosity participates in and reflects the divine attribute of righteousness-as-faithfulness-to-covenant.
6.literary_structure: The paragraph builds from principle (sow bountifully, reap bountifully) to divine ground (God makes all grace abound) to scriptural confirmation (Psalm 112:9) to divine action (God multiplies seed and harvest) to result (enrichment produces thanksgiving) to goal (glory to God and prayer for the givers). The closing doxology of v.15 brings everything to its proper terminus: not in the givers' virtue or the receivers' gratitude but in the surpassing gift of God himself. Chrysostom observes Paul's characteristic wisdom in v.10: 'after having exhorted from spiritual considerations and from temporal, in respect of the recompense also he again does the very same, making the returns he mentions of either kind — placing the two continually side by side,' so that both the spiritually-minded and the practically-minded receive their encouragement.
6.theological_application: Gill on v.7 draws the careful distinction: 'every man according as he purposeth in his heart' is not about a set sum but 'of the quality or nature of giving; or of the manner in which he is to give' — willingly, of his own accord, from an inward disposition. Matthew Henry, on the same verse, identifies three negatives Paul rules out: giving grudgingly ('as if it were wrested from him and he was sorry to part with it'), giving of necessity ('as if he were driven to it whether he will or not'), and giving out of external constraint rather than inward desire. Against these three failed modes stands the positive: God loves a cheerful giver — the Greek (hilaron) carries the sense of brightness, readiness, the disposition of someone who gives gladly because the giving itself is a pleasure. Gill on v.8 captures the divine economy: God does not merely permit sufficiency but actively multiplies what is given back so that the giver has 'all sufficiency in all things' and 'may abound in every good work' — the generosity of the creature is met by the amplifying generosity of the Creator.
6.christology: The closing doxology — 'Thanks be to God for his inexpressible gift' (v.15) — brings the entire collection argument to its christological ground. Gill identifies the 'unspeakable gift' as Christ himself: 'who, though his Son, his own Son, his only begotten Son, yet gave him for us' — the gift that cannot be adequately described or valued. The whole argument of chapters 8–9 is therefore set within a christological frame: it opens with Christ's voluntary poverty for the sake of enriching others (8:9) and closes with the inexpressible gift of Christ himself (9:15). Every act of Christian generosity is a participation in and response to that original incomparable giving: as God gave his Son, and as the Son gave himself to poverty, the people of Christ give of their material resources to those in need — producing a chain of gratitude and glory that returns to God.
